picks.headline: 40-acre Ozark parcel with proven water infrastructure
picks.reason: The only candidate combining Class 3 soil (Linker Mountainburg, 5% slopes), confirmed year-round creeks and waterfall, grid utilities already run, and existing cabins for immediate occupancy in the Tomahawk Creek–Little Red River watershed. At $396,500 ($9,912/acre) it is the most expensive per-acre pick, but no other listing eliminates build cost, water risk, and soil risk simultaneously. The trade-off is price and grid dependence—this is comfort homesteading, not off-grid pioneering.

picks.headline: 172-acre Class 2 farmland at $1,512 per acre
picks.reason: Ruston fine sandy loam (USDA Class 2, 1–3% slopes) is the best agricultural soil in the entire candidate pool, and 172 acres at $260,000 ($1,512/acre) gives production scale that no other listing approaches. No flood risk, elevation 183 ft in Dunn Creek–Champagnolle Creek watershed. The serious risks are real: no well, no septic, no structures, and the hunting-focused marketing raises a zoning question that must be resolved before purchase—residential development could be restricted.

picks.headline: 8.78-acre spring-fed parcel with Class 3 ag soil
picks.reason: A natural spring combined with Weller silt loam (USDA Class 3, farmland of statewide importance, 6.2% slopes) gives this 8.78-acre parcel the best water-plus-soil combination available under $100k. Flood zone X, well-drained, elevation 776 ft in the Pisgah Creek–Moniteau Creek watershed. At $78,900 ($8,986/acre) it is not cheap per acre, but road access and utility hookup are the only unknowns—the land fundamentals are sound for off-grid development.

picks.headline: 6-acre Jacks Fork stream parcel with ag-grade soil
picks.reason: At $45,900 ($7,537/acre) this is the lowest entry price for a Class 3 soil parcel with year-round stream access and confirmed water rights in the entire candidate pool. Same Weller silt loam and Pisgah Creek watershed as Parcel L above. The 6.09-acre size is the main constraint—small acreage limits livestock scale and resilience. Septic and utility installation are required, adding $20–40k to the true cost.

picks.headline: 26-acre Oregon County parcel at $3,037 per acre
picks.reason: Best price-per-acre among candidates with confirmed utilities and stated year-round water: 26.31 acres at $79,900 ($3,037/acre) in Oregon County, MO. Alred-Gatewood complex (Class 4, 12% slopes) rules out row crops but the acreage gives room for timber, foraging, and livestock on gentler terrain pockets. The 12% average slope means site selection for a building pad and garden requires field-walking. Trade-off: remote, steep, marginal soil—but the raw value and scale at this price are unmatched among serviced parcels.

picks.headline: 18-acre Wildcat Hill with utilities and water
picks.reason: 18 acres with utilities on-site and year-round water at $62,900 ($3,494/acre) in Oregon County, MO makes this the second-best value for a serviced mid-size parcel. Alred-Gatewood Class 4 soil at 12% slopes constrains agriculture similarly to Ruby Ridge West, but the 18 acres, flood zone X, and confirmed water give enough working room for a livestock-focused homestead. The 12% slope is the recurring practical challenge for any tillage or heavy equipment.

picks.headline: 21-acre serviced parcel, largest in the cluster
picks.reason: Sand Hollow #18 offers the most acreage (21.34 acres) of any utilities-confirmed parcel in the Bendavis-Poynor cluster at $107,900 ($5,056/acre) in Texas County, MO, elevation 1,190 ft in the Burton Branch–Big Piney River watershed. Class 4 soil with severe limitations means food production relies on raised beds and pasture rather than row crops. This listing lacks the year-round water and paved-access tags that stronger picks carry, so water source verification is critical before offer.

picks.headline: 19.57-acre Ozarks parcel near county services
picks.reason: Dog's Bluff North B at 19.57 acres and $97,900 ($5,003/acre) pairs reasonable acreage with utilities, water, and a near-services tag—useful for medical emergencies and supply runs during a homestead build-out phase. Bendavis-Poynor Class 4 soil at 5% slope is manageable for construction and grazing but not crop farming. The near-services attribute differentiates it from more isolated parcels at similar prices in the same watershed.

picks.headline: 20-acre build-ready parcel with year-round water
picks.reason: Sand Hollow #22 at 20.29 acres and $105,900 ($5,219/acre) carries build_ready and year-round water tags alongside utilities, giving slightly more confidence in immediate habitability than other large Bendavis-Poynor parcels. Class 4 soil is the persistent limitation across this cluster. At over 20 acres in the Burton Branch–Big Piney River watershed, there is enough land to experiment with soil amendment and food forest strategies even if row-crop yields will be poor.

picks.headline: 11.81-acre paved-access parcel, highest fit score
picks.reason: Dogs Bluff South #7 carries the highest homesteadFitScore (72) among the Bendavis-Poynor cluster, with paved access, confirmed water rights, and utilities at $54,900 ($4,649/acre) in Texas County. Class 4 soil at 5% slope limits farming but supports a buildable, habitable homestead. The listing notes are sparse on whether utilities are at the road or on-site—that distinction should be confirmed before closing.

picks.headline: 10.81-acre paved-access parcel, lowest cluster price
picks.reason: Shelton Road #4 at $47,900 ($4,431/acre) is the lowest-priced per-acre option in the Bendavis-Poynor group with paved access, utilities, and year-round water all tagged. The 10.81 acres at elevation 1,190 ft in the Burton Branch–Big Piney River watershed gives just enough room for a home site, small outbuildings, and modest grazing. Class 4 soil is the standard limitation here—productive gardening will require heavy soil amendment and raised beds.

picks.headline: 10.31-acre grazing parcel with water rights
picks.reason: Dogs Bluff South #3 at $49,900 ($4,840/acre) rounds out the list with explicit water rights and a grazing_suitable tag, making it the most livestock-credible of the small Bendavis-Poynor parcels. homesteadFitScore of 70 sits above most peers in the cluster. The Class 4 soil and 10.31-acre scale are genuine limitations—this works as a starter homestead for someone focusing on a few goats or chickens rather than crop self-sufficiency. Road access specifics and whether the water source is on-lot need verification.
